coredata.dc:title: Nanohybrid of Co3O4 and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots for electrochemical detection of hydroquinone
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huiying
Li, Ruiyi
Li, Zaijun
coredata.dc:description: Hybrid of metal oxide and graphene quantum dots may pave one way to improve their electronic, chemical and electrochemical properties. The paper reports one new strategy for synthesis of Co3O4-histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Co3O4-His-GQD). Here, His-GQDs are combined with Co2+ ions to rapidly produce Co-His-GQD complex. Followed by the thermal annealing at 350°C in air to form Co3O4-His-GQD. The resulting nanohybrid offers a well-defined three-dimensional architecture with the rich of porous structures. The regulation of His-GQDs on the release rate of Co2+ ions during the oxidation process of Co2+ make the formed Co3O4 crystals have a small particle size of about 22nm. The in situ synthesis of Co3O4 on the surface of graphene sheets achieves to the intimate chemical and electrical contacts of Co3O4 with His-GQDs. This creates a fast energy and electron transfer between Co3O4 and His-GQDs in the nanohybrid. The sensor based on the Co3O4-His-GQD exhibits an ultrahigh electrochemical response towards hydroquinone. Its differential pulse voltammetric peak current linearly increases with increasing hydroquinone in the range of 2×10−9–8.0×10−4 M with the detection limit 8.2×10−10 M (S/N=3). The sensitivity is better than that of both His-GQDs sensor and Co3O4 sensor. The analytical method provides the advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and stability, it has been successfully applied in the determination of hydroquinone in environmental water samples. The study also provides a promising approach for fabrication of functional graphene quantum dot-based electrode materials with high-performance for sensing, electrocatalysis, supercapacitors and lithium ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis
composite
electrochemical sensor
determination
hydroquinone

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for synthesis of Co3O4-His-GQD.
SEM image (A), enlarged SEM (B), TEM image (C) and XRD pattern of Co3O4-His-GQD.
Raman spectra of Co3O4-His-GQD (a) and His-GQD (b).
CV curves of the bare GCE (a) and the sensor based on the Co3O4 (b), A-His-GQDs (c) and Co3O4-His-GQD sensor (d) in the pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mMK2Fe(CN)6 at the scan rate of 100mVs−1.

AC impedance spectra of the sensors based on the A-His-GQDs (a), Co3O4 (b) and Co3O4-His-GQD (c) in a pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mMK4Fe(CN)6. Inset: The enlarged AC impedance spectra of the sensor based on the Co3O4-His-GQD and equivalent circuit from the EIS measurements.

Cyclic voltammograms of the Co3O4-His-GQD sensor in pH 7.0 PBS containing 1mM of K4Fe(CN)6 at various scan rates (from the inner to the outer) of 50, 70, 90, 110, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, 280, 300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 440, 460, 480 and 500mVs−1(A), plot of the Ip vs. square root of scan rate (B) and plot of the Ep vs. logv (C and D).

CV (A) and DPV curves (B) of the Co3O4-His-GQD sensor in the pH 7 PBS in the absence (a) and the presence of 1×10−4 M of hydroquinone (b) at scan rate of 100mVs−1. The DPV parameters were set to a scan rate of 4mVs−1, 50mV pulse amplitude, 20ms pulse width and −0.2V initial potential.

A: CV curves of the Co3O4-His-GQD sensor in the presence of 5×10−5 M of hydroquinone in the pH of 5.8, 6.0, 6.4, 6.6, 6.8, 7.0, 7.4, 7.6, 7.8 and 8 at scan rate of 100mVs−1 (from right to left). B: Plot of the peak potentials vs. pH.

A: DPV curves of the sensor in 5×10−4 M hydroquinone (a), 2.5×10−3 M resorcinol (b) and 5×10−3 catechol (c). B: DPV curves of the sensor in 5×10−4 M hydroquinone (a), 5×10−4 M ascorbic acid (d), 5×10−4 M dopamine (e) and 5×10−4 M hydrogen peroxide (f).
Impedance parameters calculated from equivalent circuits.
A comparison of the reported electrochemical sensors for hydroquinone.
Results for the determination of hydroquinone in water samples (N=5).

row.entry: Determined hydroquinone by the proposed method (μM)

head.ce:title: Nanohybrid of Co3O4 and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots for electrochemical detection of hydroquinone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We develop one new strategy for synthesis of Co3O4-histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots. The synthesis strategy achieves to the small particle size of Co3O4 crystals, excellent dispersion and full contacts with His-GQD. The electrochemical sensor based on the nanohybrid exhibits an ultrahigh sensitivity for the detection of hydroquinone.

Hybrid of metal oxide and graphene quantum dots may pave one way to improve their electronic, chemical and electrochemical properties. The paper reports one new strategy for synthesis of Co3O4-histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Co3O4-His-GQD). Here, His-GQDs are combined with Co2+ ions to rapidly produce Co-His-GQD complex. Followed by the thermal annealing at 350°C in air to form Co3O4-His-GQD. The resulting nanohybrid offers a well-defined three-dimensional architecture with the rich of porous structures. The regulation of His-GQDs on the release rate of Co2+ ions during the oxidation process of Co2+ make the formed Co3O4 crystals have a small particle size of about 22nm. The in situ synthesis of Co3O4 on the surface of graphene sheets achieves to the intimate chemical and electrical contacts of Co3O4 with His-GQDs. This creates a fast energy and electron transfer between Co3O4 and His-GQDs in the nanohybrid. The sensor based on the Co3O4-His-GQD exhibits an ultrahigh electrochemical response towards hydroquinone. Its differential pulse voltammetric peak current linearly increases with increasing hydroquinone in the range of 2×10−9–8.0×10−4 M with the detection limit 8.2×10−10 M (S/N=3). The sensitivity is better than that of both His-GQDs sensor and Co3O4 sensor. The analytical method provides the advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and stability, it has been successfully applied in the determination of hydroquinone in environmental water samples. The study also provides a promising approach for fabrication of functional graphene quantum dot-based electrode materials with high-performance for sensing, electrocatalysis, supercapacitors and lithium ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydroquinone is an isomer of phenolic compounds which extensively used in several fields such as cosmetics, medicines, dye, photostabilizer, plastic, antioxidant and pesticide [1]. Hydroquinone is considered to be one of the strongest inhibitors of melanin production and uses as the most effective ingredient for treating melasma [2]. However, its long-term application brings numerous adverse effects such as irritative dermatitis, melanocyte destruction, contact dermatitis and ochronosis [3]. Also hydroquinone is defined as a common environmental pollutant, because it is difficult to degrade in an ecological environment and highly toxic to human health even at low concentrations [4]. The development of sensitive and reliable analytical methods for the determination of hydroquinone is of importance. At present, main analytical methods for detection of hydroquinone are high performance liquid chromatography [5], chemiluminescence [6], spectrophotometry [7], fluorescence [8] and electrochemical methods [9]. Because of low cost, fast analysis, high sensitivity and selectivity, the electrochemical sensor based on the nanomaterial received more interest [10].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterial with the characteristics deriving from quantum dots and graphene [11]. GQDs are expected to have significantly optical properties due to its quantum confinement and edge effect, making GQDs an excellent candidate for the construction of optical materials [12]. Thus, GQDs are widely applied in optical probe [13], cellular imaging [14], light-emitting diode [15] and drug delivery [16]. Also GQDs are recognized as a good electron transporter and acceptor similar to graphene, making GQDs an optimal candidate for the fabrication of electrode materials [17]. Thus, GQDs are used as an electrode material for fuel cell [18], supercapacitor [19], photovoltaic cell [20], lithium ion battery [21] and electrochemical sensor [22]. Different from classical graphene, GQDs provide an excellent opportunity to graft them with functional groups through π-π conjugated network. The co-presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at their edge manifests high water-solubility and easy conjugation with biological moieties [23]. In addition, GQDs can also work as a multivalent redox species using cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry measurements, and present exciting opportunities for making electrochemical sensors [24]. However, the poor electronic conductivity of GQDs will greatly limit its applications in various electrochemical fields. In the recent years, great effort has been attempted to improve the electrochemical characteristics [20]. One approach is to dope heteroatoms into graphene sheets. The investigation demonstrates that the introduction of heteroatoms can adjust the intrinsic properties of GQDs, including electronic characteristics and surface and local chemical features. Herein, various nitrogen [25], boron [26], chlorine [27], phosphorus [28] and nitrogen and sulfur-co-doped GQDs [29] have been designed and investigated to construct optical and electronic devices. Another approach is to introduce some special functional groups into the edge of graphene sheets. The studies have proved that the introduction of functional groups may drastically alter the electronic characteristics, leading to unusual properties and related applications [30]. Recently, our group developed several functionalized GQDs via the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and natural amino acid [31]. These functionalized GQDs have been successfully applied in lithium ion batteries [32,33], supercapacitors [34] and sensors [35]. The results indicate that the use of functionalized GQDs accelerates the electron transfer and electrolyte transport between electrode and electrolyte, resulting in an improved electrochemical performance. Except for the doping and functionalization hybrid of GQDs and metal oxide or metal nanoparticles attracted more attention, because the hybrid potentially paves an effective method to improve their electronic, chemical and electrochemical properties [36]. Arvand and Hemmati reported the magnetic nanoparticles embedded with graphene quantum dots and multiwalled carbon nanotubes as a sensing platform for electrochemical detection of progesterone [37]. Owing to special properties of GQDs and synergistic interactions between GQDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the electrochemical sensor based on the composite provides a low detection limit (2.18nM) and high sensitivity (16.84AM−1) for progesterone detection.

As an important electroactive material, Co3O4 has been widely applied in supercapacitors [38], lithium ion battery [39], fuel cell [40], electrochemical sensor [41] and electrocatalysis [42]. Several ways were used to further improve the electrochemical activity such as the construction of Co3O4 crystals with special nanostructures [43], doping metal elements into Co3O4 crystals [42] and hybrid with other nanomaterials [44]. Long et al. reported the visible light induced photoelectrochemical properties of n-BiVO4 and n-BiVO4/p-Co3O4 [45]. The combination of n-BiVO4 with p-Co3O4 greatly speeds up the charge separation at the interface and leads to an enhanced catalytic activity. As a result, the n-BiVO4/p-Co3O4 turned out to be about 16 times more active in phenol photo oxidation than single n-BiVO4. The study also proves that hybrid of p-Co3O4 and n-type nanomaterials can be an effective approach to improve the electrochemical performance. Based on the results, we have reason to believe that, we believe that hybrid of p-type Co3O4 and n-type His-GQDs [46] largely enhances the electrochemical response towards hydroquinone N-doped GQDs. Herein, we for the first time reported one new strategy for synthesis of Co3O4-histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Co3O4-His-GQD). The as-prepared nanohybrid exhibit a well-defined three-dimensional architecture with the rich of porous structures. The synthesis strategy achieves to the small particle size of Co3O4 crystals, excellent dispersion and full contact with His-GQD. Because an excellent electrocatalytic activity was achieved, the electrochemical sensor based on the Co3O4-His-GQD provides ultrahigh sensitivity for the electrochemical detection of hydroquinone.

Hydroquinone, histidine, citric acid and cobalt chloride (CoCl2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (Mainland, China) and used without further purification. His-GQDs were prepared by using a reported method [31]. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.0, Na2HPO4-KH2PO4-NaCl-KCl, 0.01M) was prepared in the laboratory. The chitosan solution was prepared by dissolving 5.0g of chitosan in 100ml of 1.0% (v/v) acetic acid. All other reagents employed were of analytical reagent grade or with highest quality and were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Ultra pure water (18.2MΩcm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

His-GQD solid sample (0.8g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (5ml) and its acidity was adjusted to pH 7 using an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. Added the CoCl2 solution (0.25M, 10ml) into the above solution under stirring to form Co-His-GQD complex. The formed precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 1000rpm and treated by thermal annealing at 350°C in air for 4h to obtain Co3O4-His-GQD.

To evaluate the electrochemical properties of Co3O4-His-GQD, two control samples, Co3O4 and activated His-GQDs (termed as A-His-GQDs), were made in the laboratory. Here, the Co3O4 sample was prepared by the thermal annealing of Co3O4-His-GQD at 600°C in air for 6h to remove the His-GQD. The A-His-GQDs sample was obtained by the thermal annealing of His-GQDs at 350°C in air for 4h to strip the chemically active groups.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis were carried out in a HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. SEM sample was prepared by placing a drop of dilute ethanol dispersion of the composites onto a copper plate attached to an aluminum sample holder, and the solvent was allowed to evaporate at room temperature. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was conducted on a JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope at 200 keV. The sample was prepared by dispensing a small amount of dry powder in ethanol. Then, one drop of the suspension was dropped on 300mesh copper. TEM grids covered with thin amorphous carbon films. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a X-ray D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40kV and 20mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ=0.15406nm. Infrared spectrum (IR) was recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. The pH was measured on PHS-3D pH meter (Shanghai Precision Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China). His-GQD and Co-GQD solutions were imaged with a DSU live-cell confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan) system with laser excitations of DAPI, and the images were analysed using image-Pro Plus software. Raman measurements were carried out using an InVia laser micro-Raman spectrometer. The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured at 77K on a Quantachrome Nova 2000. Prior to the gas sorption measurements, all the samples were outgassed in vacuum at 120°C for 24h. The specific surface area and the pore size distribution were calculated using the Braunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and the relative pressure range of p/p0 from 0.1 to 0.3 was used for multipoint BET calculations. Non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) assuming the pores are slit/cylinder shaped was used to determine the pore size distribution and mesopore volume.

Electrochemical experiments were performed on the CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China). Conventional three electrode system was used for cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements, in which Ag/AgCI electrode (saturated KCI), platinum wire, and bare or modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 1.0mm in diameter) were used as reference electrode, counter electrode and working electrode, respectively. The DPV parameters were set to a scan rate of 4mVs−1, 50mV pulse amplitude, 20ms pulse width and −0.2V initial potential. Before use, GCE was polished successively with 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05μm alumina powder, and sonicated in a 6.0M of nitric acid/doubly distilled water and ethanol/doubly distilled water for 20min. After that, GCE as working electrode was subjected to cyclic scanning in 0.5M of sulphuric acid solution in a potential range from −0.1V to 1.0V. When the cyclic voltammogram was almost unchanged, the electrode was taken out, cleaned with ultra pure water and dried in a stream of nitrogen. The treated GCE was modified with different sensing material to prepare different sensor using a same procedure in the following: Co3O4-His-GQD was dispersed in the aqueous chitosan solution (5mgml−1) under ultrasonication to make up 2mgml−1 solution. The 10μl dispersion was dropped on the surface of GCE. The electrochemical experiment was carried out under room temperature, and all experimental solution was degassed by nitrogen for at least 15min. Then, a nitrogen atmosphere was maintained during the electrochemical measurements. For the EIS measurement a potential amplitude of ±5mV and a frequency range of 0.01 to 105 Hz were adopted.

Synthesis of Co3O4-His-GQD includes two processes (shown in Fig. 1 ). First, His-GQDs are combined with Co2+ ions to rapidly form Co-His-GQD complex. The previous research has confirmed that the His-GQD consists of tiny graphene sheets with the rich of hydroxyl, carboxyl and imidazole groups [31]. The existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl makes the His-GQD has high water-solubility. Hence, the His-GQD is easy to dissolve in water to form a transparent solution. The existence of imidazole groups makes the His-GQD can combine with Co2+ ion to form stable Co-His-GQD complex through the coordination bonds between nitrogen and cobalt. The study shows that the reaction system with 0.8g of His-GQD is transparent and no any precipitate can be observed after added the CoCl2 solution (0.25M) of less than 0.5ml. This is because the reaction is mostly occur in the interior of graphene sheet in the presence of a low Co2+ concentration. Thus, the reaction doesn’t bring an obvious increase in molecular weight of the complex and the formed complex can also keep a relatively high water-solubility. However, the reaction system begin to become turbid when the added volume of the CoCl2 solution is more than 0.5ml. In the presence of a relatively high concentration of CoCl2, many graphene sheets can be linked into a large framework network via the coordination bonds between nitrogen and cobalt, because the each graphene sheet of His-GQD often exists multiple imidazole groups. The network will rapidly extend outward to form large complex with the formation of new coordination bonds, leading to a decreased water-solubility. Based on the theory of coordination chemistry, one Co2+ ion can simultaneously combine with four nitrogen atoms to form a tetrahedral complex. On the one hand, multi-faceted growth of the complex results in the formation of quasi spherical nanoparticles. On the other hand, the molecular weight and number of the complex will rapidly increase with the increase of CoCl2 concentration. The amounts of precipitation reaches the maximum when the volume of the CoCl2 solution reaches 10ml, indicating that the reaction of Co2+ with the His-GQD is close to complete. To make full use of His-GQD in the system, the CoCl2 solution of 10ml was employed for synthesis of Co-His-GQD.

The structure of Co-His-GQD was characterized by SEM, fluorescence microscope and XRD technologies. The results were shown in Figs. s1–s3. The Co-His-GQD is composed of the nanoparticles with the average particle size of 57nm. The particle size is much bigger than that of graphene sheets (about 5.2nm) in His-GQDs. This is because the graphene sheets in His-GQDs are polymerized into the three-dimensional Co-His-GQD nanoparticles. The fluorescence microscope analysis reveals that the Co-His-GQD is almost no any fluorescence emission and its pristine His-GQDs has a strong fluorescence emission, indicating that the addition of Co2+ causes an obvious fluorescence quenching process. This verifies the formation of Co-His-GQD complex again. There only is one weak diffraction peak at 25° on the XRD patterns of Co-His-GQD and His-GQDs in Fig. s3, which corresponds to the crystal plane (002) of graphene. The result indicates that the formation of Co-His-GQD doesn’t produce new crystal faces. This is because both graphene sheets in the His-GQDs and Co-His-GQD have a relatively large size. Due to the large size of graphene sheets, four graphene sheets are difficult to simultaneously approach one Co2+ ion to form a stable Co(His-GQD)4 complex. Thus, number of the ligands in one complex mostly is less than 4. The resulted Co-His-GQD exhibits an irregular spatial structure. Due to the large size of Co(His-GQD)n, the ordered growth and directional arrangement of the Co-His-GQD complexes were greatly limited, finally leading to the failure in the formation of Co-His-GQD crystals. For the above reason, no new diffraction peaks appear on the XRD pattern of Co-His-GQD complex. In addition, we also noted that the XRD diffraction peak of graphene in the Co-His-GQD offers a weaker intensity compared with that in the pristine His-GQDs. This is due to a lower graphene sheet content in Co-His-GQD compared with His-GQDs.

The Co-His-GQD was further treated by the thermal annealing at 350°C in air to obtain Co3O4-His-GQD. In the step, the Co-His-GQD will be slowly decomposed with the oxidation of Co2+. On the one hand, the strong coordination bonds between the Co2+ ions and the imidazole groups make the release rate of Co2+ from the complex is a slow and stable process. The regulation of His-GQDs on the release rate of Co2+ helps to form small Co3O4 crystals. On the other hand, the Co3O4 crystals are generated in situ on the graphene sheets. This helps to achieve the good dispersion of Co3O4 crystals in the hybrid and the intimate contacts between Co3O4 and His-GQDs. In addition, chemically active groups in the His-GQDs will be stripped during thermal annealing such as −OH and −COOH and −NH2. With the departure of these chemically active groups, the distance between graphene sheets and Co3O4 crystals in the hybrid will be greatly shortened, improving the electron and energy transfer between the Co3O4 and the His-GQD in the hybrid.

The structure of Co3O4-His-GQD was characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, IR and Raman technologies. Fig. 2 presents typically the SEM image, TEM image and XRD pattern of Co3O4-His-GQD. The Co3O4-His-GQD offers a well-defined three-dimensional architecture with high porosity similar to that of Co-His-GQD complex. The unique structure possesses a large specific surface area, providing abundant active sites for the catalytic reaction events, and big pore volume, accelerating the mass transfer of redox species. To verify the above suggest, the specific surface area and pore-size distribution of Co3O4-His-GQD were measured by nitrogen adsorption/desorption experiment and the results were shown in Fig. s4. The BET surface area reaches to 162m2 g−1, indicating a large specific surface area. The large specific surface area is mainly attributed to the small particle size of Co3O4 crystals and the introduction of His-GQDs. The pore size distribution spans from 18 to 1000nm. Here, the macro-pores could originate from the interconnected hollow spaces in the Co-His-GQD and channels left by the gas escapes during the thermal annealing. The micro-pores could attribute to the structural rearrangements during conversion of Co-His-GQD complex into Co3O4-His-GQD. The enlarged SEM and TEM images further reveal that the formed Co3O4 crystals have a small particle size of 22nm, excellent dispersion in the hybrid and full contact with tiny graphene sheets. The above characteristics help to realize the synergistic effect in electrochemistry between Co3O4 and His-GQD. On the XRD pattern of Co3O4-His-GQD there are six main diffraction peaks. All diffraction peaks in Fig. s4 can be indexed to the cubic spinel Co3O4 phase (JCPDS data card No.43-1003), indicating the formation of cubic spinel Co3O4 without impurities. The main characteristics peaks at 19.2°, 30.9°, 36.2°, 38.3°, 44.8° and 55.7° correspond to the (111), (220), (311), (222), (400) and (422) crystal planes of cubic spinel Co3O4, respectively. Fig. s4 also shows that these diffraction peaks of Co3O4 crystals are intense and sharp, indicating a good crystallization. The above results demonstrate that the well-crystalline Co3O4 crystals can be formed by the thermal annealing in air at 350°C. Except for Co3O4 crystals His-GQDs is another important component in the Co3O4-His-GQD. However, the characteristic diffraction peak of graphene does not appears in the XRD pattern of Co3O4-His-GQD. Often, graphene has one weak and wide diffraction peak at about 25°. Compared with classical graphene, His-GQDs has a lower degree of crystallization due to its small size and lower content of graphene sheets in the Co3O4-His-GQD. These factors further reduce the intensity of diffraction peak of graphene. The diffraction peak of graphene is very weak relative to strong diffraction peaks of Co3O4 crystals, so we cannot observe its presence in Fig. s4.

The IR spectra of His-GQD and Co3O4-His-GQD were shown in Fig. s5. There are several IR absorption peaks and bands on the IR spectrum of Co3O4-His-GQD. The band between 3600cm−1 and 3300cm−1 is the symmetric stretching vibration IR absorption of −NH in the amino and −OH in the hydroxyl and carboxyl. The peak at 1700cm−1 could be assigned to the CO stretch vibration. The peak at 1380cm−1 could be attributed to CH stretching vibration, suggesting the presence of carbonyl and CH group. The peak at 1150cm−1 could correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO bond. Fig. s5 also indicates that the IR spectrum of Co3O4-His-GQD is very similar with that of pristine His-GQD. The result demonstrates that main functional groups in the His-GQD can be partly maintained during the thermal annealing at 350°C.

Fig. 3 shows the Raman spectra of Co3O4-His-GQD and His-GQD. There are two typical Raman peaks of graphene materials on the Raman spectrum of His-GQD, including D band (1341cm−1) and G band (1601cm−1). The D band is attributed to an A1g vibration mode of carbon atoms with a double-resonance process in plane terminations of disordered graphite. The G band arises from the E2g mode of graphitic carbon and is assigned to the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the graphite layer. Two characteristic Raman peaks of Co3O4-His-GQD shifted to 1381 and 1601cm−1. That is, the G band energy remained virtually unchanged, whereas the D band energy showed a substantial blue-shift of 40cm−1. The result verifies that the energy transfer between the His-GQD and the Co3O4 was achieved in the hybrid, which is analogous to hole doping that has been found to lead to an increase of the D band frequency of graphene [47].

To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of Co3O4-His-GQD, three kinds of the sensors based on the Co3O4, A-His-GQDs and Co3O4-His-GQD were made by using the same procedure. The CV behavior of bare GCE and three sensors were investigated in a pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mMK4Fe(CN)6 at the scan rate of 100mVs−1. Fig. 4 shows that the each of bare GCE and three sensors can offer a pair of redox peaks on the CV curve. The result demonstrates that the oxidation and reduction of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− can carry out on all electrode surfaces. The Co3O4 sensor has a higher peak current with a smaller peak potential compared with the bare GCE. This is attributed to the special catalytic capacity of Co3O4. The catalysis of Co3O4 accelerates the electrochemical reaction process and results in a more sensitive CV response, indicating an increased peak current on the CV curve. Similar to the Co3O4 sensor, the A-His-GQD sensor gives a higher peak current on the CV curve compared with the bare GCE. This is because the His-GQDs has a larger specific surface area compared with the classical graphene due to its smaller size. The large surface area provides more catalytic active sites for the reduction and oxidation of Fe(CN)6 3−/4−, leading to a higher CV peak current. The Co3O4-His-GQD sensor exhibits the highest CV peak current and smallest peak potential among three sensors, verifying that the Co3O4-His-GQD has the best electrocatalytic activity. This is mainly attributed to the hybrid of Co3O4 and His-GQD. As Co3O4 is a p-type semiconductor [45] and His-GQD is a n-type semiconductor [46], the hybrid of Co3O4 and His-GQD forms a type of n-/p-junction on the interface. The formation of n-/p-junction greatly accelerates the charge separation at the interface and leads to an enhanced electrocatallytic activity. Thus, the Co3O4-His-GQD sensor can provide a more sensitive CV response compared with the Co3O4 sensor and the A-His-GQD sensor.

Electrochemical impedance behavior of of the sensors based on the different sensing materials in a pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mM of K4Fe(CN)6 was studied to understand the results of CV analysis. Fig. 5 presents that Nyquist spectrum of the each sensor includes one semicircle in high frequency and a subsequent inclined line at the low frequency end. The semicircle in high frequency region is related to the ionic conduction through the electrolyte and electronic conduction between the substrate and the active material. The sloping straight line at low frequency is corresponded to the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− diffusion in the bulk materials. The pattern of EIS can be fitted by the equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 5. In the equivalent circuit, Rs is the bulk electrolyte resistance, Rct is the electron and charge transfer resistance, Ci is the interfacial capacitance, and Zw is Warburg resistance related to a combination of the diffusional effects of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− ion on the interface between the active material particles and electrolyte, which is generally indicated by a straight sloping line at low frequency end. In general, the combination of Rct and Zw is called faradic impedance, which reflects kinetics of the electrode reaction, and a low Rct corresponds to a fast kinetics of the faradic reaction. Table 1 shows that the Rct of Co3O4 sensor is remarkably lower than that of His-GQD sensor. The fact verifies that the Co3O4 has a much better electronic conductivity compared to His-GQD. The Rct will greatly reduce if Co3O4 was replaced by Co3O4-His-GQD. The value is much lower that of Co3O4 sensor and His-GQD sensor, indicating an excellent electronic conductivity. The improved electronic conductivity could mainly attribute to the interconnected network structure of Co3O4-His-GQD and a small particle of Co3O4 crystals. The former facilitates a much faster charge transport across graphene sheet junctions. The later reduces the distance of electron transfer during the electrode reaction, and increase the specific surface area, providing abundant active sites for the catalytic reduction events. The above factors result in an excellent electronic conductivity of Co3O4-His-GQD.

Based on the results of electrochemical impedance measurement, the diffusion coefficients of Fe(CN)6 4− (D) in the modified layer were calculated by the Eq. (1) [48]:
(1)
D =
R
2
T
2
2 A
2
n
4
F
4
C
2
σ
2
where R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, n is the number of electrons transferred, A is the surface area of the electrode, F is the faraday constant, and C is the concentration of Fe(CN)6 4−. The value of parameter σ is the slope of real part of impedance (Zre) versus inverse square root of radial frequency (ω−1/2) in the Warburg region. The D value for different sensor was calculated by using the Eq. (1). Table 1 shows that A-His-GQDs sensor exhibits the lowest diffusion coefficient among four sensors, indicating a slow kinetic process of electrode reaction. This is because the graphene sheets of A-His-GQDs offer a small size and the rich of active groups. The small size makes graphene sheets are easy to enter into the interior of the pores formed by the chitosan coagulation during the sensor preparation. The active groups can strongly interacte with chitosan to denser chitosan film. Such a film will partly block the electrolyte transport in the modified layer on the electrode surface, thus leading to a slow diffusion process. The diffusion coefficient will increase to more than 8 times if the A-His-GQDs was replaced by Co3O4. This is mainly attributed to the three-dimensional structure of Co3O4. The special structure brings a much bigger pore volume compared with two-dimensional His-GQDs, providing a fast mass transfer of Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. Table 1 shows that the diffusion coefficient of Co3O4-His-GQD reaches to 2.24×10−4 cm2 s−1. The value is more than 16 times that of Co3O4 sensor and 130 times that of A-His-GQD sensor, indicating a faster kinetic process of electrode reaction. This is contributed to large specific surface, big pore volume and structural stability of Co3O4-His-GQD and active groups of His-GQD. The large specific surface area provides more abundant active sites for the catalytic reaction events, the big pore volume accelerates the mass transfer of electrolyte in the modified layer on the electrode surface, and the structural stability greatly reduce the loss of the specific surface area and the channel blockage caused by the collapse of porous structures during the sensor preparation and the electrode reaction. The active groups of His-GQD make it easy to combine with Fe(CN)6 3−/4− to realize a faster electrolyte transport during the electrode reaction. In addition, the spongy His-GQD layer on the surface of Co3O4 crystals works as an ion reservoir to contain a host of the electrolyte, shortening the transport path of Fe(CN)6 3−/4−.

To study on the dynamic behavior of electrode reaction, the CV curves of Co3O4-His-GQD sensor in a pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mM of K4Fe(CN)6 were investigated at different scan rates. Fig. 6 A shows that the peak current will rapidly increase with the increase of scan rate. However, the peak potential is nearly independent of the scan rate. The result demonstrates that the Co3O4-His-GQD mediator has been efficiently connected on the GCE surface for facile charge transfer. Both cathodic and anodic peak currents are linearly proportional with the scan rate. This verifies that the electrode reaction corresponds to the solution phase is a quasi-reversible electrochemical process. According to the results of CV, the apparent heterogeneous electron transfer for Fe(CN)6 4− within the Co3O4-His-GQD film was estimated by Laviron’s model. Fig. 6D presents the relationship plot of peak-to-peak separation value (ΔE p) with logarithm values of scan rate (logv). A small ΔE p for all scan rates was observed, indicating fast electron transfer rate. Because the nΔE p is lower than 200mV, the apparent heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k s) is estimated by the following equation [49]: k s =mnFv/RT, where m is a parameter related to peak-to-peak separation; T is the temperature; n is the number of electrons; and v is the scan rate. The k s was found to be 16.3±0.25s−1 and the value is obviously better than that of both Co3O4 sensor (6.1±0.10s−1) and His-GQD sensor (1.76±0.13s−1), indicating fast electron transfer of Fe(CN)6 4− solution to the electrode.

Fig. 7 presents the CV and DPV curves of Co3O4-His-GQD sensor in the pH 7.0 PBS in the absence and presence of hydroquinone. No any redox peaks can be seen from the CV curve in the absence of hydroquinone, verifying that the Co3O4-His-GQD doesn’t occur the redox reaction at the potential between −0.8 and 0.8V. Thus, the Co3O4-His-GQD only works as a catalyst for speeding up the redox reaction of hydroquinone on the electrode surface and itself does not participate in the electrode reaction. In the presence of hydroquinone, a pair of strong redox peaks at −0.07V and 0.223V appeared in the CV curve. The reversible redox peaks is attributed to the oxidation and reduction of hydroquinone on the electrode surface. Owing to high electrocatalytic activity, the Co3O4-His-GQD accelerate the electrode reaction and leads to an enhanced peak currents. Fig. 7B presents DPV curve of the sensor in the absence and presence of hydroquinone. In the absence of hydroquinone is almost a straight line without any current peak. Thus, the reaction medium cannot interfere with the detection of hydroquinone by using the DPV. In the presence of hydroquinone, the DPV curve appears a strong DPV peak. Because DPV gives a better current response and resolution compared with CV, it was employed for the detection of hydroquinone.

Hydroquinone is a weak acid due to existence of hydroxyl groups in the molecule. Its protonation and deprotonation in water may influence on the electrochemical behavior on the electrode surface. To examine the effect of the acidity in the medium on the detection of hydroquinone, the CV curves of the sensor in the PBS with different pH from 5.8 to 8.0 were measured in the presence of 1.0×10−5 M of hydroquinone. The result was shown in Fig. 8 . It can be seen from Fig. 8A that the peak current of hydroquinone on the CV curve decreases with the increase of pH when the pH is less than 6.8. This is mainly because the hydroxyl in hydroquinone is easy to combine with proton in a weak acid solution and form positively charged hydroquinone isomer. The charged hydroquinone isomer has a stronger adsorption capacity on the electrode surface compared with hydroquinone molecule due to strong electrostatic attraction with the Co3O4-His-GQD, thus leading to a strong peak current signal on the CV curve. The number of positively charged hydroquinone isomers rapidly decreases with the increase of pH and free hydroquinone molecules increases. This will reduce the adsorption capacity of hydroquinone on the electrode surface, thus leading to a reduced peak current on the CV curve. However, the peak current of hydroquinone on the CV curve increases with the increase of pH when the pH is higher than 6.8. This is mainly because the hydroxyl in hydroquinone is easy to dissociate into negatively charged hydroquinone isomer in a weak base solution. The negatively charged hydroquinone isomer is unstable in a relatively strong base solution and easy to be oxidized into benzoquinone, thus leading to strong peak current signal on the CV curve. Of course, it must be pointed out that pH in the medium will also affect on the dissociation and electrocatalytic activity of the His-GQD and Co3O4 on the electrode surface. Therefore, CV behavior of the sensor at the pH depends on a variety of factors come from hydroquinone and Co3O4-His-GQD. As a result, the variation of the peak current on the CV curve with the pH in the medium does not completely accord with the above simple rules.

The relationship curves between pH and the peak potential for the anode is presented at Fig. 8B. The equations for the peak potential with pH are Ep =−0.0598pH+0.5983 (mV, R2 =0.999). From the above equation, we noted that the slopes is close to the theory value of 59.2mV/pH. The result demonstrates that the uptake of electrons is accompanied by an equal number of protons for each of the isomers. The electron transfer numbers (n) for hydroquinone were calculated by based on the relationship between E p and pH. The result was found to be 2, indicating that the redox reaction of hydroquinone at the sensor may be a two electron and two proton process.

The Co3O4-His-GQD sensor was used for electrochemical detection of hydroquinone. In these measurements, the concentration of hydroquinone was only changed, while the other conditions were kept constant. The DPV response of the sensor was investigated in pH 7 PBS containing different concentration of hydroquinone. Fig. 9 A presents DPV curves of the sensor in pH 7.0 PBS containing different concentration of hydroquinone. It can be seen from Fig. 9A that the peak current on the DPV curve increases with the increase of hydroquinone concentration. Further, Fig. 9B indicates that the peak current (I p) is linear to the concentration of hydroquinone in the range of 2.0×10−9–8.0×10−4 M. The corresponding linear regression equations is expressed in the following equation: I p =0.024C+0.752, with statistically significant correlation coefficient of 0.9997, where I p is peak current intensity in the DPV in μA and C is the concentration of hydroquinone in the unit of μM. The detection limit was 8.2×10−10 M for hydroquinone that was obtained from the signal-to-noise characteristics of these data (S/N=3). The sensitivity is much better than that of the Co3O4 sensor (1.2×10−5 M) and His-GQDs sensor (2.5×10−5 M). The enhanced sensitivity for the Co3O4-His-GQD sensor demonstrates that hybrid of Co3O4 and His-GQD leads to a significantly electrochemical synergy. In addition, the sensitivity is obviously higher than that of the reported electrochemical sensors for hydroquinone in Table 2 . Such a high sensitivity could be attributed to its unique structure and composition of Co3O4-His-GQD. The His-GQD in the hybrid offer a larger specific surface area compared with classical graphene due to its small size of graphene sheets, providing more catalytic active sites, the rich of chemically active groups such as OH, −NH2 and imidazole, providing more electroactive atoms, large electron conjugated systems including imidazole and graphene sheets, providing a fast electron transfer rate. The Co3O4 itself is a good electro-catalyst. As our synthesis strategy creates a small particle size of Co3O4 crystals, the larger specific surface area and higher conductivity make the Co3O4 has an enhanced electro catalytic activity towards hydroquinone. More importantly, our synthesis strategy successfully achieves to an intimate chemical and electrical contact of Co3O4 with His-GQDs. Because His-GQDs is a n-type semiconductor and Co3O4 is a p-type semiconductor, the combination creates one n/p-type heterojunction on the interface, which will greatly accelerate the charge separation between the Co3O4 and the His-GQDs in the hybrid. The above factors will largely improve the electrocatalytic activity and bring a more sensitive electrochemical response to hydroquinone and leads to an ultrahigh sensitivity for the detection of hydroquinone.

To investigate the reproducibility of sensor batch production, fifty Co3O4-His-GQD sensors were fabricated using the same Co3O4-His-GQD and preparation procedure. Their DPV responses were measured in a pH 7 PBS containing 1.0×10−6 M of hydroquinone at 20°C, respectively. Relative standard deviation (RSD%) of the DPV responses was found to be 1.9, verifying good reproducibility. For the same sensor, it was used for successive detection of 1.0×10−6 M of hydroquinone. The RSD of the sensor in fifteen successive detection of hydroquinone is 2.4%, indicating high repeatability. The sensor was also examined by storing it in a place at 4°C for six weeks and the DPV peak current can remain 95.2% of the originally measured value at least, indicating a high stability.

The anti-interference also is a significant parameter of the electrochemical sensor for the detection of hydroquinone in environmental water samples. For environmental water samples, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, CI−, CO3 2−, PO4 3− and SO4 2− are main inorganic ions. These ions are not electrically active, their existence can not cause any electrochemical response under the optimum conditions for detection of hydroquinone. The investigation shows that the presence of more than 1000-fold above inorganic ions only causes a change of less than 1% on the peak current of DPV for 5×10−6 M hydroquinone. Although Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ may carry out the redox reaction under the conditions for detection of hydroquinone. However, these exists in environmental water samples at a very low level. The investigation indicates that 100-fold above ions only causes a change of less than 2.2% on the peak current of DPV for 5×10−6 M hydroquinone. In waste water samples, catechol and resorcinol are most likely to interfere with the electrochemical determination of hydroquinone due to their similar structure and chemical properties. To test the effect of catechol and resorcinol on the detection of hydroquinone, the DPV behaviors of catechol, resorcinol and hydroquinone were investigated and the result was shown in Fig. 10 A. It can be seen from Fig. 10A that the DPV curve of resorcinol almost is a straight line, indicating a very weak DPV response. Catechol gives a sensitive DPV response similar to hydroquinone, but their peak potentials on the DPV curves exist in an obvious difference. The investigation shows that 10-fold resorcinol and 5-fold catechol don’t interfere with the detection of hydroquinone. In addition, ascorbic acid, dopamine and hydrogen peroxide may exist in environmental water at a very low level. Hence, ascorbic acid, dopamine and hydrogen peroxide are likely to interfere with detection of hydroquinone due to their high electrochemical activity. Fig. 10B presents DPV curves of the sensor in the pH 7.0 PBS in the presence of 10-fold ascorbic acid, dopamine and hydrogen peroxide. Compared with hydroquinone, ascorbic acid, dopamine and hydrogen peroxide give a much lower DPV responses. Further investigation confirms that the additions of 10-fold ascorbic acid, dopamine and hydrogen peroxide only caused the changes of 1.4%, 4.7% and 1.2% in the DPV signal of hydroquinone, respectively. Based on the experimental results above, it is obvious that the Co3O4-His-GQD sensor reduces the influence of the possible interfering species and exhibits an excellent sensing performance towards the detection of hydroquinone.

The Co3O4-His-GQD sensor was applied in the determination of hydroquinone in environmental water samples. The spike and recovery experiments were performed by measuring the DPV responses to the sample, in which the known concentrations of hydroquinone were added. The concentration of hydroquinone in water sample was determined from the calibration curve and the value was used to calculate the concentration in the original sample. The mean±SD of each sample and recovery of each spiked sample were calculated, and the values are reported in Table 3 . The recovery of hydroquinone for spiked samples analysis is in the range of 98.0-104.0%. These indicated proposed method has a good accuracy and precision.

We have demonstrated synthesis of Co3O4-histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and its application as an ultrasensitive electrochemical platform for detection of hydroquinone. The study reveals that the hybrid of Co3O4 and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots helps to achieve a fast energy and electron transfer due to forming a n-/p-junction at the interface. The combination also induces a significantly electrochemical synergy for the oxidation and reduction of hydroquinone on the electrode surface. The sensor based on the nanohybrid exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity for electrochemical detection of hydroquinone. The excellent electrocatalytic activity make the nanohybrid can be widely used as a novel sensing platform for fabrication of electrochemical sensors. The study also provides a promising approach for the fabrication of functional graphene quantum dots-based electro active materials with high-performance for sensing, catalysis, supercapacitors and lithium ion batteries.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115). Prospective Joint Research Project: Cooperative Innovation Fund (No. BY2015019-26) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.09.174.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
